PUBLIC
UNITED STATES OF AMERICA
FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION
OFFICE OF ADMINISTRATIVE LAW JUDGES
__________________________________________
)
In the Matter of )
)
Dr. Donald McCrosky, ) Docket No. 9448
)
Appellant. )
__________________________________________)
ORDER ON APPLICATION FOR REVIEW AND STAY OF SANCTIONS
This case arises from sanctions imposed against Dr. Donald McCrosky, a
Veterinarian, after an Arbitrator found he committed nine violations of the Rules
adopted to implement the Horseracing Integrity and Safety Act (“HISA”).1 Five of
the violations were for Possession of Banned Substances found during a search of
Dr. McCrosky’s minivan at a racetrack subject to HISA. Two others were for
Tampering, and one other for Use of a Banned Substance. The ninth violation was
for Trafficking. Dr. McCrosky, a Covered Person under HISA, has filed a Notice of
Appeal and Application for Review, as well as an Application for a Stay of the
sanctions imposed. The Horseracing Integrity and Safety Authority (the
“Authority”) timely filed individual responses to Dr. McCrosky’s Review and Stay
Applications. (respectively, the Authority’s “Review Resp.” and “Stay Resp.”).
1 See generally 15 U.S.C. §§ 3051-60. Capitalized terms used, but not defined, in this Order, are from
HISA Rule 1020 of the General Provisions and the Rules adopted under HISA, unless otherwise
identified.

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 2 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
This Order resolves both matters.
I. Summary of the Regulatory Structure and the Relevant Rules.
The Authority has adopted, and the FTC has approved, various rules to
implement HISA, including those comprising the statutorily-required Anti-Doping
and Medication Control (“ADMC”) Program.2 The Horseracing Integrity & Welfare
Unit (“HIWU”) enforces these rules on behalf of the Authority.3 Violations of
various Rules implementing the ADMC Program provide for sanctions, which can
include a period of Ineligibility to participate in HISA-governed thoroughbred
racing, a fine, payment of adjudication, and publication of the violation.4
The HISA and ADMC Program rules address, among other subjects: (1) the
substances that are banned outright or subject to threshold presence requirements
or are regulated as controlled medications; (2) the conduct constituting violations
and corresponding sanctions; and (3) the sanctions that may be imposed for Rule
violations. The more serious violations are Anti-Doping Rule Violations (“ADRVs”),
which include Banned Substances that “should never be in a horse’s system.”5 To
2 15 U.S.C. §§ 3053, 3055, 3057. See generally ADMC Rule 3000 Series; 88 Fed. Reg. 27894 (Mar. 27,
2023) (FTC Notice of Final Rule, effective May 22, 2023), https://hisaus.org/regulations?modal-
shown=true#equine-anti-doping-and-controlled-medication-protocol-rules.
3 Rule 3010(e)(1).
4 See, e.g., Rule 3212. HISA Rule 1020 defines “the penalties resulting from the occurrence of one or
more [Rule] violations” as “Consequences,” which parties often refer to as “sanctions,” a term also
used, but not defined, in the Rules. For simplicity, I use the term “sanctions.”
5 Rule 3010(c). See also Rules 1020 (definition of ADRV), 3111(a) (Prohibited Substances).
2

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 3 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
implement the Program, the Authority has issued a Prohibited List of Banned
Substances.6
II. Summary of the Underlying Facts.
A. The Nine ADRV Charges Against Dr. McCrosky.
In April 2025, HIWU investigators searched Dr. McCrosky’s minivan, which
serves as his “mobile veterinary unit,” while parked at Fairmount Park Racetrack
in Collinsville, Illinois.7 They discovered five Banned Substances:
1. Testosterone cypionate/DHEA, an anabolic agent. Testosterone is classified
as an S1 Banned Substance (anabolic agents), as well as a Controlled Substance
under federal regulations.8
2. Thyro-l, a metabolic hormone, used to treat insufficient thyroid
production.9 Thyro-l is an S4 Banned Substance (hormone and metabolic
modulators).10
3. Ammonium sulfate, a nerve-blocker. Ammonium sulfate is an S0 Banned
Substance (non-approved).11
6 See Rules 1020 (definition of Prohibited List), 3111(a), 4010-4117 (Banned Substances), Appendix 1
to Rule Series 4000 (Technical Document—Prohibited Substances).
7 Amended Final Decision at ¶¶ 2.3-.4, 2.24.
8 Rule 4112(a). See Matter of Overly, No. 9443, at 4-6 (FTC ALJ Jan. 27, 2026) (quoting authority)
https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/614746.2026.01.27_administrative_
law_judge_decision_on_application_for_review.pdf, app. for review, FTC (Feb. 26, 2026).
9 Amended Final Decision at 37; HIWU v. Poole, JAMS Case No. 1501000576, at ¶¶ 2.19, 5.2 (Aug. 8,
2023), aff’d sub nom. Matter of Poole, No. 9417, 2023 WL 8435860, at *5 (FTC ALJ Nov. 13, 2023).
10 Appendix 1 to Rule Series 4000.
11 Id. & Amended Final Decision at ¶ 6.3.2.c.
3

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 4 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
4. P-Bloc (ammonium chloride), also a nerve blocker, and an S0 Banned
Substance.12
5. Osphos, a bisphosphonate, that can be used to strengthen bones and treat
navicular disease, lameness or arthritis.13 Osphos is an S6 Banned Substance
(miscellaneous).14
Rule 3214(a) prohibits “Possession of a Banned Substance . . . unless there is
compelling justification for such Possession.” The Rule imposes strict liability,
regardless of knowledge or intent, absent an exception that is inapplicable here.15
HIWU subsequently charged Dr. McCrosky with five violations of the ADMC
Program’s Possession rule.
That was only the beginning of Dr. McCrosky’s exposure to ADMC Program
rule violations, however. Interviewed by HIWU the day of the search, Dr. McCrosky
admitted administering testosterone to Tigger Attack, a Covered Horse owned by
Dr. McCrosky’s wife.16 HIWU thereafter charged Dr. McCrosky with a “Use”
violation.
Rule 3213(a) prohibits “Use . . . of a Banned Substance . . . in relation to a
Covered Horse . . . . The success or failure of that Use . . . is not material.” “Use
means the utilization, application, ingestion, injection, or consumption by any
12 Id.
13 Amended Final Decision at 37 & ¶ 2.4. See Matter of Shell (“Shell II”), No. 9439, 2025 WL
1784696, at *24 (FTC ALJ Mar. 6, 2025).
14 Rule 4117(a).
15 Rule 1020 (definition of Possession). See Shell II, 2025 WL 1784696, at *11, n.16; Poole, JAMS
Case 1501000576, at ¶¶ 7.7-.8.
16 Amended Final Decision at ¶¶ 2.3, 2.6, 2.7.
4

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 5 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
means whatsoever of any Prohibited Substance . . . in relation to a Covered
Horse.”17 Subsection (b) further provides that a Covered Person has a “personal and
nondelegable duty . . . to ensure that no Banned Substance . . . is Used in relation to
his or her Covered Horse.” Covered Persons are “therefore strictly liable for any Use
of a Banned Substance,” and “it is not necessary to demonstrate intent, Fault,
negligence, or knowing Use . . . to establish” the Rule 3213 ADRV.
In addition, Dr. McCrosky repossessed the thyro-l and osphos that HIWU
seized as evidence.18 He stated that he needed each Banned Substance to service
clients who were “not associated with the Fairmount Park racetrack.”19 In
consequence, he was charged with “Tampering.” Under Rule 3216(a), “Tampering
. . . with any part of Doping Control or Medication Control” includes “intentional
conduct that subverts the Doping Control or Medication Control process, . . .
includ[ing] . . . any . . . interference . . . with any aspect of Doping Control or
Medication Control.”20
Events after the HIWU search produced yet more charges. HIWU later
alleged another Tampering ADRV arising from allegedly false statements
Dr. McCrosky made to HIWU, and conduct he undertook, after another of his wife’s
Covered Horses, Childersattack, tested positive for testosterone.21 Besides covering
17 Rule 1020 (definition).
18 Amended Final Decision at ¶¶ 1.2, 6.3.5.
19 Id. at 37 & ¶ 6.3.2.a & b.
20 Rule 1020 (definition).
21 Amended Final Decision at ¶ 2.8.
5

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 6 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
“subvert[ing] the Doping Control or Medication Control process,” Tampering also
includes “falsifying documents submitted to [HIWU] [or] committing any . . .
fraudulent act upon [HIWU] . . . to affect Results Management or the imposition of
Consequences [that is, sanctions] . . . .”22
Finally, HIWU discovered two filled syringes in the tack room of a Fairmount
Park trainer, Isidoro Castro. Mr. Castro said Dr. McCrosky sold him the syringes,
which later forensic analysis revealed contained two Banned Substances. HIWU
charged Dr. McCrosky with Trafficking.23
Rule 3214(b) prohibits “Trafficking . . . in any Banned Substance . . . .”
“Trafficking” means, in relevant part, “a Covered Person selling, giving,
transporting, sending, delivering, or distributing by any means a Banned Substance
. . . to any other Person . . . .” There is, however, a definitional exception for conduct
by Veterinarians “involving a Prohibited Substance [which include Banned
Substances] used for genuine and legal therapeutic purposes or other acceptable
justification.”24
B. The Sanctions Associated with the Nine ADRV Charges.
The sanctions available for the charged ADRVs here differ:
(1) Possession and Use ADRVs carry an Ineligibility period of “2 years” for a
first offender; (2) Tampering has more severe sanctions: “4 years” of Ineligibility for
first offenders, with exceptions for non-intentional conduct or for “exceptional
22 Rule 1020 (definition).
23 Amended Final Decision at ¶¶ 2.19-.23.
24 Rule 1020 (definition of “Trafficking”).
6

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 7 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
circumstances”; and (3) Trafficking similarly provides for a “[m]inimum of 4 years
. . . .”25 There are also monetary fines for each ADRV: (a) for Possession and Use,
“up to $25,000 or 25% of the total purse (whichever is greater)”, and (b) for
Tampering and Trafficking, “up to $50,000 or 50% of the total purse (whichever is
greater).”26 All the ADRVs also provide for an assessment of “some or all of the
adjudication costs and [HIWU’s] legal costs.”27
ADRVs afford a Covered Person two additional defenses: (1) No Fault or
Negligence (“NF”); and (2) No Significant Fault or Negligence (“NSF”). If a Covered
Person proves there was NF on their part, then Rule 3224 eliminates sanctions
entirely. If NSF is proven, sanctions may be reduced, albeit not eliminated under
Rule 3225. Both NF and NSF are limited to “exceptional circumstances.” 28
Dr. McCrosky has conceded that NF consideration does not apply, however.”29 NSF
is analyzed under a “tiered” structure that defines three NSF severity levels.30 Also,
in Possession cases to date, I have analyzed these defenses product-by-product.31
25 Rule 3223(b).
26 Id.
27 Id.
28 Rule 3224(b) (NF). See Rule 1020 (definition of NSF, which must be “viewed in the totality of the
circumstances and taking into account the criteria for [NF] . . . .”); Overly, No. 9443, at 84 (quoting
authority).
29 Amended Final Decision at ¶ 8.40.
30 See Cilic v. International Tennis Federation, CAS 2013/A/3327 (Apr. 11, 2024), which is commonly
used in HISA cases).
31 See, e.g., Overly, No. 9443 at 70-84.
7

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 8 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
Various other sanctions “wrinkles” are potentially relevant where, as here, multiple
ADRVs are alleged.32
C. The Arbitration Proceeding.
An arbitration was begun to adjudicate the nine charged ADRVs, and an
evidentiary hearing was conducted on January 14, 2026.33 Dr. McCrosky’s defense
to the five Possession charges focused on the Rule 3214(a)’s “compelling
justification” provision. Briefly, he maintained that the Banned Substances found
were intended for veterinary use “off-track,” that is, only with non-Covered
Horses.34 Dr. McCrosky asserted that administered testosterone to Covered Horse
Tigger Attack, which his wife owned, because the horse was “not eating well” and
that he inadvertently failed to administer it sufficiently far “in advance of a race”—
a claimed “act . . . of Negligence rather than a willful violation.”35
The two Tampering charges, discussed in greater detail later in this Order,
arose from different circumstances. Dr. McCrosky maintained that he repossessed
the thyro-l and osphos “by perceived necessity” to have the Banned Substances
available to treat non-Covered Horses owned by two clients.36 The other Tampering
charge concerned Dr. McCrosky’s statements and conduct regarding castration of
Childersattack, also owned by his wife. He alleged that, in substance, that HIWU
32 See Shell II, 2025 WL 1784696, at *33-34 (charging authority and sanction proportionality);
Overly, at 85-95 (consecutive Ineligibility and aggregated monetary sanctions).
33 Amended Final Decision at ¶¶ 3.6, 3.15-.18.
34 Id. at ¶¶ 6.3.1-.2.
35 Id. at ¶ 6.3.4.
36 Id. at ¶ 6.3.5.
8

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 9 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
did not meet its burden of proof.37 On the Trafficking charge, also discussed below,
Dr. McCrosky asserted that the syringes sold were supposed to contain Controlled
Medications, not Banned Substances, to be used for “genuine and legal therapeutic
purposes.”38
HIWU sought individual sanctions for each ADRV charged, with consecutive
Ineligibility periods and aggregated fines.39 Dr. McCrosky maintained that NSF
analysis should be applied to reduce any applicable sanction and that the
proportionality principle should preclude the consecutive Ineligibility and
aggregated fines HIWU sought.40
Based on the witness testimony at the evidentiary hearing and the other
evidence admitted, the Arbitrator held that HIWU proved all nine ADRVs. The
Arbitrator rejected Dr. McCrosky’s compelling justification defense, noting that his
factual showing “has not come close to discharging his high burden of establishing”
this exception to Possession.41 Dr. McCrosky’s defense to the Use ADRV failed, as
“Banned Substances are prohibited at all times and cannot be used on a Covered
Horse [here, Tigger Attack] under any circumstances.”42
The Arbitrator similarly rejected Dr. McCrosky’s defenses to the two
Tampering ADRVs: (1) repossession of the thyro-l and osphos after their seizure
37 Id. at ¶ 6.3.6.
38 Id. at ¶ 6.3.7.
39 Id. at ¶¶ 6.2(mm), (pp)-(ss).
40 Id. at ¶¶ 6.3.9 & 11-12.
41 Id. at ¶ 8.20.
42 Id. at ¶ 8.22.
9

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 10 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
“reflect[ed] a disdain or disregard for the rules of the ADMC program”; while (2)
Dr. McCrosky “deliberately attempted to mislead the authorities regarding the
status of Childersattack.”43 HIWU also proved the Trafficking ADRV. HISA and
Illinois Racing Board (“IRB”) Rules prohibited “any person, other than a
veterinarian, to possess syringes or other injectables on racetrack grounds,” and
Dr. McCrosky “admitted” knowing that “syringes [were] not permitted” at
Fairmount Park Racetrack where, he also “knew . . . Trainer Castro would use
[them] . . . on Covered Horses.”44
Finally, the Arbitrator rejected Dr. McCrosky’s NSF argument finding that:
“Dr. McCrosky acted with intent, and with a flagrant disregard of the ADMC
Program Rules . . . . His actions were wide ranging, they were flagrant and they
involved a number of Covered horses.”45 He further held that, although
“proportionality must be taken into consideration,” Dr. McCrosky’s “intentional and
egregious conduct . . . warrants the imposition of the maximum allowable fines and
a consecutive period of Ineligibility commensurate with the sanctioned conduct.”46
Accordingly, the Arbitrator awarded a 24-year period of Ineligibility and a $300,000
fine. He also directed that Dr. McCrosky pay all adjudication costs (with the amount
43 Id. at ¶¶ 8.25 & 8.31.
44 Id. at ¶¶ 8.35, 8.36, 8.37.
45 Id. at ¶¶ 8.43 & 8.45.
46 Id. at ¶¶ 8.48 & 8.50.
10

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 11 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
undermined in the decision).47 The Arbitrator further directed HIWU to publish his
decision in accordance with applicable Rules.48
D. Dr. McCrosky’s Review Application and the Authority’s Response.
Dr. McCrosky alleges five errors in the prior proceeding and Arbitrator’s
decision:
1. Imposing consecutive Ineligibility periods, resulting in 24 years of
Ineligibility, disregards the proportionality principle applicable to the
ADMC Program.
2. The Trafficking ADRV relied on unreliable evidence and was not
established to the comfortable satisfaction standard of proof.
3. The Arbitrator’s standard for showing compelling justification under Rule
3214(a) is impracticably high.
4. HIWU failed to prove the Childersattack Tampering ADRV by direct
evidence of fraudulent intent.
5. By denying Dr. McCrosky’s request for a continuance, which necessitated
his appearing virtually, the Arbitrator violated his due process rights.
In its response, enforcement counsel asserts that:
1. Rule 3228(d) permits the consecutive Ineligibility periods awarded, which
the Arbitrator reasonably declined to reduce on proportionality grounds.
47 Id. at ¶¶ 8.51 & 9.1(b)-(d).
48 Id. at ¶ 9.1(e).
11

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 12 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
2. The Trafficking violation was not based on “a single self-serving hearsay
statement,” but rather on the totality of the evidence, including
Dr. McCrosky’s own testimony and the Arbitrator’s credibility assessment.
3. The Arbitrator adopted the correct standard for establishing compelling
justification and correctly found Dr. McCrosky failed to meet it.
4. The Arbitrator correctly held that, by submitting false information to
HIWU regarding Childersattack, Dr. McCrosky intentionally subverted
the Doping Control process, thereby committing a Tampering ADRV.
5. The Arbitrator’s denial of Dr. McCrosky’s continuance request, along with
Dr. McCrosky’s virtual participation, satisfied due process.
Besides seeking review of the Arbitrator’s decision and the sanctions
imposed, Dr. McCrosky also seeks a stay of the sanctions. I will consider stay relief
first. After that, I address the review application and the further proceedings in this
case.
III. Dr. McCrosky’s Motion for a Stay.
Under FTC Rule 1.148(c) & (d), an application for a stay of the sanctions
imposed “must provide the reasons a stay is . . . warranted by addressing the
[following] factors . . . and the facts relied upon”:
(1) The likelihood of the applicant’s success on review;
(2) Whether the applicant will suffer irreparable harm if a stay is not
granted;
(3) The degree of injury to other parties or third parties if a stay is granted;
and
12

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 13 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
(4) Whether the stay is in the public interest.49
On a stay motion, “[t]he first two factors of the traditional standard are the most
critical.”50 Moreover, “[a] stay pending appeal is an intrusion into the ordinary
processes of administration and judicial review, so this extraordinary relief is never
granted as a matter of right.”51
A. Summary of the Parties’ Positions.
Dr. McCrosky contends that his review proceeding “presents substantial,
non-frivolous grounds for reversal or modification on multiple fronts.”52 First, he
argues that the Arbitrator should not have imposed “fully consecutive maximum
sentences on all nine ADRVs” and modification of “consecutive sentencing structure
presents a strong likelihood of success.”53 Second, he maintains that the Arbitrator
erred in rejecting his “compelling justification” defense to the five Possession
ADRVs.54
On irreparable injury, Dr. McCrosky asserts that the 24-year Ineligibility
sanction amounts to “a permanent lifetime ban,” and that absent a stay, he will
sustain injury in the form of lost “professional time, client relationships, [and]
49 87 Fed. Reg. 60078. See generally Nken v. Holder, 556 U.S. 418, 434 (2009) (adopting comparable
factors).
50 Nken, 556 U.S. at 434.
51 Rhode Island State Council of Churches v. Rollins, No. 25-2089, 2025 WL 3135862, at **5 (1st Cir.
2025) (cleaned up), admin. stay extended, No. 25A539, 2025 WL 3145875 (mem.) (U.S. Nov. 11,
2025). See also Media Matters for Am. v. FTC, No. 25-5302, 2025 WL 2988966, at *3 (D.C. Cir. Oct.
23, 2025) (A stay “pending appeal is an extraordinary form of relief.”) (cleaned up).
52 Stay App. at 1.
53 Id. at 1-2.
54 Id. at 2.
13

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 14 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
practice capacity.”55 Public disclosure of the Authority’s sanctions, he further
argues, “compounds this harm” and causes “reputational damage . . .
permanent[ly].”56 In addition, Dr. McCrosky maintains that “[l]ost income” from
roughly 50% of his practice (presumably that involving Covered Horses) will not be
“compensable after the fact.”57
Dr. McCrosky contends that the balance of equities and public interest favor
a stay. His inability to service Covered Horses at Fairmount Park Racetrack is said
to “reduce[] veterinary coverage” and impair “equine welfare interests.”58 Absent a
stay, the review process provided for in HISA will, in his view, be “rendered
meaningless.”59
Opposing stay relief, the Authority argues that Dr. Scott’s likelihood of
“overall success on review is “low.”60 On the nine ADRVs that the Arbitrator found
proven, the minimum per violation sanction is two-years Ineligibility, and
Dr. McCrosky simply is unlikely to overturn liability on all of the ADRVs.61
According to the Authority, the economic harm Dr. McCrosky may experience is not
irreparable since half of Dr. McCrosky’s practice involves non-Covered Horses.
Other elements of harm are, the Authority asserts, “speculative”; prior public
55 Id. at 2.
56 Id.
57 Id. at 3.
58 Id.
59 Id.
60 Stay Resp. at 3.
61 Id. at 3-4.
14

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 15 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
disclosure of Dr. McCrosky’s ADRVs is no longer remediable by a stay.62 The
Authority also contends a stay would harm other “Responsible Persons,” referring to
Veterinarians and other Covered Persons who follow the Rules.63 It also would
thwart “the ADMC Program’s mission to protect horse welfare” and “the integrity of
horseracing.”64 Comparable considerations, the Authority asserts, similarly inform
the public interest, which “is served by individual compliance with the rules and
regulations validly promulgated by [regulatory] agencies.”65
* * *
Preliminarily, I emphasize that, in considering the merits of this proceeding,
my review is de novo—“as if it had not been heard before, and as if no decision
previously had been rendered”— and will be based on the full record, recently
filed.66 At this point, however, I am ruling only on the papers filed on
Dr. McCrosky’s review and stay applications, and the Authority’s responses to those
two filings.
62 Id. at 5.
63 Id.
64 Id.
65 Id. at 6.
66 Freeman v. DirecTV, Inc., 457 F.3d 1001, 1004 (9th Cir. 2006) (review under Federal Rule of Civil
Procedure 12(b)(6)). See also Harris v. Lincoln Nat’l Life Ins. Co., 42 F.4th 1292, 1295 (11th Cir.
2022) (“De novo means . . . a fresh, independent determination of the ‘matter’”) (quoting with
approval Doe v. United States, 821 F.2d 694, 697-98 (D.C. Cir. 1987)); Reyes-Colón v. United States,
974 F.3d 56, 60 (1st Cir. 2020) (“review . . . de novo . . . is a legalistic way of saying we critique the
judge’s decision without giving any deference to his views”); Amparan v. Lake Powell Car Rental
Cos., 882 F.3d 943, 947 (10th Cir. 2018) (“[A] district court’s grant of summary judgment [is
reviewed] de novo,” and “[i]n so doing, we need not defer to factual findings rendered by the district
court.”) (internal quotation marks omitted); Aquarius Marine Co. v. Pena, 64 F.3d 82, 87 (2d Cir.
1995) (on de novo review, the appellate court “give[s] no deference to the lower court”).
15

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 16 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
B. Likelihood of Success.
Likely success on the merits must be “better than negligible.”67 On this
factor, Dr. McCrosky’s burden is uncommonly heavy, as the Arbitrator found he
committed nine ADRVs and prescribed individual sanctions for each, to run
consecutively. Because any individual ADRV calls for at least a two-year minimum
Ineligibility period, unless Dr. McCrosky offers a plausible argument that applies to
the overall 24-year Ineligibility sanction imposed, stay relief is largely academic.
Dr. McCrosky does not provide sufficient facts to establish a reasonable
likelihood that each of the nine ADRVs will have to be overturned. The Arbitrator’s
failure to grant a continuance seems unlikely to have violated due process on the
facts here. “[S]elf-imposed scheduling obstacles” rarely justify granting an
arbitration continuance.68
However, Dr. McCrosky also contends that imposing consecutive Ineligibility
periods for each ADRV violates the sports law principle, adopted in HISA cases, of
proportionality: “[I]t is a widely accepted general principle of sports law that the
severity of a penalty must be in proportion with the seriousness of the
67 Nken, 556 U.S. at 434. See also Citizens for Resp. & Ethics in Washington v. FEC, 904 F.3d 1014,
1019 (D.C. Cir. 2018) (“Crossroads’ appeal shows little prospect of success—an arguably fatal flaw for
a stay application.”).
68 Scott v. Prudential Sec., Inc., 141 F.3d 1007, 1017 (11th Cir. 1998). See also Amended Final
Decision at ¶¶ 3.11-.17; Bartlit Beck LLP v. Okada, 25 F.4th 519, 524 (7th Cir. 2022) (citing
authorities); El Dorado Sch. Dist. No. 15 v. Cont'l Cas. Co., 247 F.3d 843, 848 (8th Cir. 2001)
(multiple grounds existed for denying a continuance); Laws v. Morgan Stanley Dean Witter, 452 F.3d
398, 400 (5th Cir. 2006) (a “conclusory motion” was insufficient to grant a continuance).
16

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 17 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
infringement.”69 But application of the principle is fact-based.70 And on his stay
motion, Dr. McCrosky has not demonstrated that, even if the proportionality
analysis were applied, the total remaining Ineligibility period would be sufficiently
reduced to make stay relief meaningful.
Helpful guidance here is found in cases such as United States v. Powell,71
where the Eighth Circuit, sitting en banc, considered whether a defendant convicted
of multiple criminal offenses could be released pending appeal. To decide the matter
of release, the court must consider, among other things, whether the case “raise[s] a
substantial question of law or fact likely to result in reversal or an order for a new
trial.” A “substantial question” affecting only a single count is, however, not enough:
[T]he substantial question . . . if decided in defendant’s favor, [must] lead to a
reversal or an order for new trial on all counts on which imprisonment has
been imposed. (An argument that would produce a reversal of fewer than all
such counts would be insufficient in this context, because if one count
imposing imprisonment survives, the reason for allowing bail pending appeal,
that a defendant should not be imprisoned under a legally erroneous
sentence, disappears.)72
This “all counts” gloss on the required “substantial question” is settled law.73
And its underlying rationale applies where a Covered Person such as Dr. McCrosky
undertakes to show likelihood of success on a stay motion: where HIWU has proven
69 Shell II, 2025 WL 1784696, at *33-34 (quoting W. v. FEI, CAS 99/A/246, at ¶ 31 (May 11, 2000)).

71 761 F.2d 1227 (8th Cir. 1985).
72 Id. at 1230, 1233 (emphasis added).

2025); United States v. Fakhuri, No. 20-cr-20162-3, 2025 WL 957510, at *2 (E.D. Mich. Mar. 31,
2025).

multiple ADRVs, unless the review would reduce the Ineligibility period imposed to

the stay—that the Covered Person should not bear the hardships of Ineligibility
“under a legally erroneous” sanction—“disappears.”
Take, as examples, the Tampering and Trafficking ADRVs, which have
Ineligibility periods of up to four years. The Arbitrator found three distinct
violations.
1. Tampering: Thyro-L and Osphos.
After HIWU discovered thyro-l and osphos during its search and seized both
Banned Substances, Dr. McCrosky repossessed them.74 According to Dr. McCrosky,
Katie Nelson, a client “not associated with the Fairmount Park racetrack,” owned a
horse “low on thyroid” and was “running out” of thyro-l.75 At the time of the HIWU
search, Dr. McCrosky was “about to take” the Banned Substance to Ms. Nelson.76
Regarding the osphos, Dr. McCrosky similarly claimed another client, Michelle
Crowder, also “not associated with Fairmount Park,” had an “urgent need for [the
osphos] the following day.”77 He also “emphasized the cost $300.”78
After hearing the evidence, the Arbitrator found that:

gelding (a fully castrated male horse).84 HIWU sought details regarding the
castration from Mr. Childers.85 In response, Dr. McCrosky sent HIWU “a
handwritten note” stating that the horse “was castrated in March of 2022,” and

Based on the evidence, the Arbitrator concluded:
(1) “[I]t [was] highly unlikely that Childersattack had functional testicular
material at the time of sample collection on October 14, 2024. This means that the
horse was not a ridgling with an undescended testicle in his body and the excess
Testosterone in his system was not endogenous”99;
(2) Dr. McCrosky’s explanation concerning the horse’s castration was
“extremely far-fetched,” permitting an inference that “Dr. McCrosky knowingly
submitted false documentation in order to explain away a possible [ADRV]”100;
(3) “On his own evidence, Dr. McCrosky admitted to knowingly
misrepresenting the horse’s status so that he would not have to file the necessary
paperwork later on”101;
(4) “There is no record of the surgery that Dr. McCrosky is said to have
performed on Childersattack”102; and
(5) Dr. McCrosky “deliberately attempted to mislead the authorities
regarding the status of Childersattack.”103

3. Trafficking.
The Trafficking ADRV arises from HIWU’s discovery of two syringes
containing glaucine and testosterone, both Banned Substances, which were
seemingly concealed in a barrel in the Fairmount Park tack room of Isidoro Castro,
a trainer.106 Mr. Castro told HIWU he purchased the syringes from Dr. McCrosky
off-track in a parking lot for $20.00.107
Dr. McCrosky admitted giving trainer Castro the syringes in a parking lot,
but testified they contained banamine and aspirin to treat a horse that was
experiencing a muscle disorder known as “tying up.’”108 The sale, he maintained,
was for “genuine and legal therapeutic purpose[s].”109 Dr. McCrosky said he did not
know the syringes contained Banned Substances.110 However, he admittedly knew

Even if he were to prevail on all six other ADRVs, Dr. McCrosky simply has

this entire review proceeding. This stay factor tips decidedly against stay relief.
4. The Six Other ADRVs Charged.
Equally significant, however, the situation does not change if I consider, at
this point, the five Possession and Use ADRVs that HIWU has charged.
Dr. McCrosky has offered no facts, as opposed to argument, suggesting material
error in the Arbitrator’s findings on any of the alleged ADRVs. Considering the
sheer number of ADRVs subject to review, and the other issues that may properly
be raised, it will be a tall order, even on de novo review, for Dr. McCrosky to prevail
on all six other ADRVs charged. While Dr. McCrosky’s compelling justification
argument applies to six ADRVs, his factual showing—which the Arbitrator found

did “not come close” to meeting his burden—is unlikely to result in overturning all

Therefore, on the papers now before me, the prospect that Dr. McCrosky will

resolve this entire review proceeding is low. So, again, this stay factor does not favor
relief.
C. Irreparable Injury.
Claimed revenue loss from the part of Dr. McCrosky’s practice involving
Covered Horses is generally insufficient to establish irreparable injury: “Mere
injuries, however substantial, in terms of money, time and energy necessarily
expended in the absence of a stay, are not enough.”120 However, any income lost as a
result of the sanctions comes from “approximately fifty percent of [his] practice,”
leaving his ability to service non-Covered Horses intact.121 He further asserts,
without fact elaboration, that the sanctions imposed will affect “[e]stablished client
relationships and track access . . . .”122 But that bare assertion is not enough:
“speculative allegations” are insufficient.”123
119 Amended Final Decision at ¶ 8.20. See generally id. at 8.4-.19; Overly, No. 9443 at 22-60; Shell II,
2025 WL 1784696, at *11-24; Scott v. Horseracing Integrity & Safety Authority, No. 2:25-cv-632-
SMD-GJF, 2025 WL 2987598, at *7 (D.N.M. Oct. 22, 2025).
120 Sampson v. Murray, 415 U.S. 61, 90 (1974), quoting with approval Virginia Petroleum Jobbers
Ass’n v. Federal Power Comm’n, 259 F.2d 921, 925 (D.C. Cir. 1958). See also Los Angeles Mem’l
Coliseum Comm’n v. NFL, 634 F.2d 1197, 1202 (9th Cir. 1980) (“alleged [monetary] injury . . . does
not usually constitute irreparable injury”) (cleaned up).
121 Stay App. at 3.
122 Id.
123 Wisconsin Gas Co. v. FERC, 758 F.2d 669, 674 (D.C. Cir. 1985).

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 27 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
The alleged harm arising from the Authority’s publishing the determination
that Dr. McCrosky has been sanctioned does not demonstrate irreparable injury. As
the Authority argues, Rule 3620 requires public disclosure of the Arbitrator’s
decision. Resulting reputational harm claimed to arise from public disclosure
therefore cannot, standing alone, justify a stay; a more probing factual presentation
must be offered. Any arguable harm has already occurred and thus would be
unaffected by issuing a stay. Thus, for example, in Lukezic v. FINRA, the Court
declined to find irreparable injury, arising from publication of a pending
professional disciplinary proceeding, where FINRA was required “to make
information about ‘disciplinary proceedings’ available in a ‘readily accessible
electronic or other process’[].”124
D. Injury to Others and the Public Interest.
The third and fourth stay factors also weigh against granting a stay. Stay
relief pending review is “extraordinary”—the exception, not the rule.125 The ADMC
Program protects the safety and integrity of horseracing and the confidence of its
124 No. 25-cv-00623 (DLF), 2025 WL 2305859, at *1, 4 (D.D.C. Aug. 10, 2025), quoting 15 U.S.C.
§ 78o-3(i)(1), (5), appeal filed, No. 25-5327 (D.C. Cir. Sept. 11, 2025). See also Doe v. Ohio State
Univ., 136 F. Supp. 3d 854, 871 (S.D. Ohio 2016) (police report was “already a matter of public
record” and thus “irrelevant to the question of irreparable injury”); Foster v. Cantil-Sakauye, No. 17-
cv-02122-JSW, 2017 WL 6886326 at *4 (N.D. Cal. Aug. 21, 2017) (no irreparable injury where
professional discipline had previously been publicly disclosed). Cf. Roudachevski v. All-American
Care Ctrs., Inc., 648 F.3d 701, 703, 706-07 (8th Cir. 2011) (although a healthcare facility notified its
residents that the plaintiff, a physician, was terminated as a facility provider, no irreparable injury
was proven).
125 See, e.g., KalshiEX LLC v. CFTC, 119 F.4th 58, 63 (D.C. Cir. 2024).
27

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 28 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
stakeholders—including not only the public but also rivals themselves—in the
fairness of thoroughbred racing competition.126
Dr. McCrosky has the burden of offering facts that inform the Court’s
exercise of discretion in his favor despite these weighty considerations. He asserts
that he is “one of only two trackside veterinarians at Fairmount Park,” and that his
inability to service Covered Horses at the racetrack would “implicat[e] the equine
welfare interests HIWU exists to protect.”127 However, Dr. McCrosky has not shown
that other veterinarians able to replace him are unavailable. Incentivizing other
veterinarians to enter to provide the services Dr. McCrosky will not be able to offer
pending this review is preferable, inasmuch as Dr. McCrosky’s showing of likely
success is weak.128
Dr. McCrosky also asserts that the FTC review process would be
“meaningless if career-ending sanctions take permanent effect before it can run.”129
But that argument could be made whenever a Covered Person receives consecutive
Ineligibility periods for multiple ADRVs. Where, as here, likelihood of success on

the merits cannot be demonstrated, it has no independent traction under the public
interest factor. It is, therefore, insufficient to grant a stay.
On the other hand, the final two stay factors favor the Authority. The
effectiveness of the ADMC Program, and the interests of both thoroughbred
horseracing participants and the public are best served by denying a stay.130
* * *
Dr. McCrosky has not met the burden set forth in FTC Rule 1.148 to grant a
stay during the pendency of this review proceeding. His application is DENIED.
IV. The Review Application.
FTC Rule 1.146(c)(2) provides that:
In reviewing the final civil sanction and decision of the Authority, the
Administrative Law Judge may rely in full or in part on the factual record
developed before the Authority through the disciplinary process under 15
U.S.C. 3057(c) and disciplinary hearings under Authority Rule Series 8300.
The record may be supplemented by an evidentiary hearing conducted by the
Administrative Law Judge to ensure each party receives a fair and impartial
hearing. Within 20 days of the filing of an application for review, based on
the application submitted by the aggrieved party or by the Commission and
on any response by the Authority, the Administrative Law Judge will assess
whether:
(i) The parties do not request to supplement or contest the facts found
by the Authority;
(ii) The parties do not seek to contest any facts found by the Authority,
but at least one party requests to supplement the factual record;
(iii) At least one party seeks to contest any facts found by the
Authority;
130 See Stay Resp. at 5-6.
29

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/31/2026 OSCAR NO. 615162 -PAGE Page 30 of 34 * PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
(iv) The Commission, if it filed the application for review, seeks
supplementation of the record; or
(v) In the Administrative Law Judge’s view, the factual record is
insufficient to adjudicate the merits of the review proceeding.131
Neither Dr. McCrosky nor the Authority has requested an evidentiary
hearing. Consequently, I hereby determine under Rule 1.146(c)(2) that the parties
do not seek to supplement the facts found by the Authority. Accordingly, I decide
this proceeding on the arbitration record and the parties’ briefs on this review.

served on the other party and contain references to the record and authorities
on which they rely.” See FTC Rule 1.146(c).

PUBLIC
14. Reply findings of fact must set forth the opposing party’s proposed finding
of fact in single space and then set forth the reply in double space.
a. Reply findings of fact must be numbered to correspond to the opposing
party’s finding that the reply finding is refuting and must use the
same outline headings used by the opposing party in its opening
proposed findings of fact.
b. If you have no specific response to, or do not disagree with, the
opposing party’s proposed finding of fact, set forth the opposing party’s
proposed finding of fact in your reply finding of fact, and then state
that you have no specific response or do not disagree.
c. The opposing party’s findings of fact must not be included in the word
count applicable to reply findings of fact.
34
